title: Blood in Cat Urine: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Noticing blood in your cat's urine? Learn about hematuria, urgency levels, and why documenting the symptoms helps your veterinarian triage.
content_html:
What is Blood in Cat Urine?

Finding drops of blood in your cat's urine, a condition known as hematuria, indicates that there is inflammation or injury somewhere in the urinary tract. This can occur in the kidneys, the bladder, or the urethra. In cats, this is frequently associated with Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD), which can be caused by infections, bladder stones, or even stress-induced inflammation.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for blood in the urine is Medium. While seeing blood is distressing, it is generally not an immediate life-threat if your cat is still behaving normally and successfully passing urine. However, you should schedule a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours. Please note: if your cat is straining and unable to produce any urine, this is a life-threatening emergency known as a urinary blockage, and you must seek help immediately.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If you are able to safely do so, taking a clear photograph of the urine drops can be a vital tool for your veterinarian. The specific shade of the blood—ranging from light pink to dark tea-colored—and the presence of any visible clots or mucus help the vet determine the severity and potential source of the bleeding before they even begin their physical examination.

faqs.question: Can stress cause blood in a cat's urine?
faqs.answer: Yes, stress can lead to Feline Idiopathic Cystitis (FIC), a condition where the bladder wall becomes inflamed and bleeds without a bacterial infection being present.

faqs.question: What should I do if my cat is also crying while using the litter box?
faqs.answer: Vocalizing while urinating suggests pain. While blood alone is a medium urgency, signs of significant pain should prompt a more immediate call to your local clinic or an emergency vet.

faqs.question: Will blood in the urine go away on its own?
faqs.answer: While some inflammatory episodes may seem to resolve, the underlying cause usually requires medical treatment or dietary changes to prevent recurrence or worsening.
